title: ST 558 HW 4 - Jon’s version
text:
Task 1

library(httr)

Warning: package 'httr' was built under R version 4.5.2

library(tidyverse)

#loading API data on Apple from top headlines
API_data <- GET("https://newsapi.org/v2/top-headlines?q=Apple&country=us&apiKey=d5aec3291c0a4f7485565a95febd8f8d")
str(API_data)

#bringing in the jsonlite package to parse
library(jsonlite)

Warning: package 'jsonlite' was built under R version 4.5.2



Attaching package: 'jsonlite'

The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    flatten

parsed <- fromJSON(rawToChar(API_data$content))

#converting the Apple data from top headlines into a tibble
Apple_data <- tibble(
    source_name = parsed$articles$source$name,
    author      = parsed$articles$author,
    title       = parsed$articles$title,
    url         = parsed$articles$url,
    publishedAt = parsed$articles$publishedAt,
    content     = parsed$articles$content
  )

#looking at the new Apple data tibble
Apple_data

# A tibble: 1 × 6
  source_name author           title                   url   publishedAt content

1 MacRumors   Hartley Charlton Craig Federighi Explai… http… 2026-06-10… Apple …

#parsing data again
library(jsonlite)
parsed <- fromJSON(rawToChar(API_data$content))

#creating the Tesla tibble from the API data
Tesla_data <- tibble(
    source_name = parsed$articles$source$name,
    author      = parsed$articles$author,
    title       = parsed$articles$title,
    url         = parsed$articles$url,
    publishedAt = parsed$articles$publishedAt,
    content     = parsed$articles$content
  )

#checking the new tibble on the Tesla data
Tesla_data

# A tibble: 0 × 0

parsed <- fromJSON(rawToChar(API_data$content))

NASA_data <- tibble(
    source_name = parsed$articles$source$name,
    author      = parsed$articles$author,
    title       = parsed$articles$title,
    url         = parsed$articles$url,
    publishedAt = parsed$articles$publishedAt,

title: ST 558 HW 4 - Jon’s version
text:
Task 2

#setting working directory for roller coaster data and reading it in to R
setwd("C:/Users/davenportjm/Documents/repos")
roller_data <- read_csv("635USrollercoasters.csv") 

Rows: 635 Columns: 21
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (12): Coaster, Park, City, State, Census Region, AgeGroup, Scale, Type, ...
dbl  (9): Year Opened, TopSpeed, MaxHeight, Drop, Length, Duration, # of Inv...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

#converting to a tibble and checking the data
roller_data <- as_tibble(roller_data)
str(roller_data)

tibble [635 × 21] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
 $ Coaster        : chr [1:635] "Adrenaline Peak" "Adventure Express" "Afterburn" "Aftershock" ...
 $ Park           : chr [1:635] "Oaks Amusement Park" "Kings Island" "Carowinds" "Silverwood Theme Park" ...
 $ City           : chr [1:635] "Portland" "Mason" "Charlotte" "Athol" ...
 $ State          : chr [1:635] "Oregon" "Ohio" "North Carolina" "Idaho" ...
 $ Census Region  : chr [1:635] "West" "Midwest" "South" "West" ...
 $ Year Opened    : num [1:635] 2018 1991 1999 2008 2010 ...
 $ AgeGroup       : chr [1:635] "3:Newest" "2:Recent" "2:Recent" "3:Newest" ...
 $ TopSpeed       : num [1:635] 45 35 62 65.6 22 67 35 66 48 50 ...
 $ MaxHeight      : num [1:635] 72 63 113 192 25 ...
 $ Scale          : chr [1:635] "Extreme" "Thrill" "Extreme" "Extreme" ...
 $ Type           : chr [1:635] "Steel" "Steel" "Steel" "Steel" ...
 $ Design         : chr [1:635] "Sit Down" "Sit Down" "Inverted" "Inverted" ...
 $ Drop           : num [1:635] 70 47 110 177 22.5 170 20 147 80 144 ...
 $ Length         : num [1:635] 1050 2963 2956 1204 628 ...
 $ Duration       : num [1:635] 66 140 167 92 47 190 100 143 110 110 ...
 $ Inversions?    : chr [1:635] "Yes" "No" "Yes" "Yes" ...
 $ # of Inversions: num [1:635] 3 0 6 3 0 6 0 0 0 4 ...
 $ Make           : chr [1:635] "Gerstlauer Amusement Rides GmbH" "Arrow Dynamics" "Bolliger & Mabillard" "Vekoma" ...
 $ Latitude       : num [1:635] 45.5 39.4 35.3 47.9 28 ...
 $ Longitude      : num [1:635] -122.7 -84.3 -80.8 -116.7 -82.6 ...
 $ Boundary       : chr [1:635] "{\"type\":\"Feature\",\"properties\":{\"CENSUSAREA\":95988.013,\"NAME\":\"Oregon\",\"GEO_ID\":\"0400000US41\",\"| __truncated__ "{\"type\":\"Feature\",\"properties\":{\"CENSUSAREA\":40860.694,\"NAME\":\"Ohio\",\"GEO_ID\":\"0400000US39\",\"L"| __truncated__ "{\"type\":\"Feature\",\"properties\":{\"CENSUSAREA\":48617.905,\"NAME\":\"North Carolina\",\"GEO_ID\":\"0400000"| __truncated__ "{\"type\":\"Feature\",\"properties\":{\"CENSUSAREA\":82643.117,\"NAME\":\"Idaho\",\"GEO_ID\":\"0400000US16\",\""| __truncated__ ...

#creating object with roller data and mutating data to update Age Group names without the numbers. Also converting other data to factors
roller_data <- roller_data |>
  mutate(
    AgeF= factor(AgeGroup, levels = c("1:Older", "2:Recent", "3:Newest"),
                  labels = c("Older", "Recent", "Newest")),
    ScaleF     = as.factor(Scale),
    TypeF      = as.factor(Type),
    DesignF    = as.factor(Design),
    InversionF = as.factor(`Inversions?`)
  )

# one-way using table for roller coaster data
table(roller_data$AgeF)


 Older Recent Newest 
    69    190    376 

#2 way using filter and table 
roller_data |>
  filter(AgeF == "Newest") |>
  select(ScaleF, TypeF)|>
  table()

         TypeF
ScaleF    Steel Wood
  Extreme   126   21
  Family    120    5
  Kiddie     14    0
  Thrill     79   11

# 2 way table using group and summarize then followed by using pivot wider 
roller_data |>
  group_by(AgeF, ScaleF) |>
  summarize(count = n())

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'AgeF'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.


# A tibble: 12 × 3
# Groups:   AgeF [3]
   AgeF   ScaleF  count
   <fct>  <fct>   <int>
 1 Older  Extreme    17
 2 Older  Family     13
 3 Older  Kiddie      5
 4 Older  Thrill     34
 5 Recent Extreme   105
 6 Recent Family     40
 7 Recent Kiddie      9
 8 Recent Thrill     36
 9 Newest Extreme   147
10 Newest Family    125
11 Newest Kiddie     14
12 Newest Thrill     90

roller_data |>
  group_by(AgeF, ScaleF) |>
  summarize(count = n()) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = ScaleF, values_from = count)

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'AgeF'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.


# A tibble: 3 × 5
# Groups:   AgeF [3]
  AgeF   Extreme Family Kiddie Thrill
  <fct>    <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>
1 Older       17     13      5     34
2 Recent     105     40      9     36
3 Newest     147    125     14     90

#creating graphs after storing initial as an object for each one then adding specific plot parameters

#1st plot is bar graph of roller coasters by age group. 

#More recent or newer roller coasters than older ones in the dataset. 

g1 <- ggplot(data=roller_data, aes(x = AgeF))
g1 + geom_bar() +
  labs(title = "Roller Coaster by Age Group",x = "Age of Roller Coaster")







# 2nd plot is bar graph with roller coasters by age and using the fill function for scale factor. 

#Not only are there more recent roller coasters by there are more in the family category for the most recent ones. 
g2 <- ggplot(data=roller_data, aes(x = AgeF, fill = ScaleF))
g2 + geom_bar() +
  labs(title = "Roller Coaster Scale by Age Group", x = "Age of Roller Coaster") +
  scale_fill_discrete("Scale")







#numeric variables with means and sd for Top Speed and Max Height of roller coaster data
roller_data |>
  summarize(mean_TopSpeed = mean(TopSpeed, na.rm = TRUE), sd_TopSpeed = sd(TopSpeed, na.rm = TRUE), mean_MaxHeight = mean(MaxHeight, na.rm = TRUE), sd_MaxHeight = sd(MaxHeight, na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  mean_TopSpeed sd_TopSpeed mean_MaxHeight sd_MaxHeight
          <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>        <dbl>
1          42.2        19.4           76.8         61.5

#numeric variables of Top Speed and Max Height filtered by Age and Scale categorical groups
roller_data |>
  group_by(AgeF, ScaleF) |>
  summarize(mean_TopSpeed = mean(TopSpeed, na.rm = TRUE), sd_TopSpeed = sd(TopSpeed, na.rm = TRUE), mean_MaxHeight = mean(MaxHeight, na.rm = TRUE), sd_MaxHeight = sd(MaxHeight, na.rm = TRUE))

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'AgeF'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.


# A tibble: 12 × 6
# Groups:   AgeF [3]
   AgeF   ScaleF  mean_TopSpeed sd_TopSpeed mean_MaxHeight sd_MaxHeight
   <fct>  <fct>           <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>        <dbl>
 1 Older  Extreme          53.6        6.09           94.9        24.1 
 2 Older  Family           25.2       13.0            33.4        23.9 
 3 Older  Kiddie           16.2       18.9            21.2        21.7 
 4 Older  Thrill           40.4       13.0            62.2        22.1 
 5 Recent Extreme          55.1       10.2           119.         49.1 
 6 Recent Family           23.1       11.3            31.4        28.0 
 7 Recent Kiddie           10.2        4.22           12.2         3.61
 8 Recent Thrill           38.1        9.79           61.9        20.8 
 9 Newest Extreme          58.6       15.3           136.         65.9 
10 Newest Family           21.4       10.9            24.9        20.8 
11 Newest Kiddie           11.4       11.9            18.3        30.9 
12 Newest Thrill           34.9        7.96           54.4        22.4 

# numeric by Age and Scale
roller_data |>
  group_by(AgeF, ScaleF) |>
  summarize(mean_TopSpeed = mean(TopSpeed, na.rm = TRUE), sd_TopSpeed = sd(TopSpeed, na.rm = TRUE), mean_MaxHeight = mean(MaxHeight, na.rm = TRUE), sd_MaxHeight = sd(MaxHeight, na.rm = TRUE))

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'AgeF'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.

# Groups:   AgeF [3]
   AgeF   ScaleF  mean_TopSpeed sd_TopSpeed mean_MaxHeight sd_MaxHeight
   <fct>  <fct>           <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>        <dbl>
 1 Older  Extreme          53.6        6.09           94.9        24.1 
 2 Older  Family           25.2       13.0            33.4        23.9 
 3 Older  Kiddie           16.2       18.9            21.2        21.7 
 4 Older  Thrill           40.4       13.0            62.2        22.1

#correlation between Top speed and Max height
#Big surprise, there is a 0.903 positive relationship with Top Speed of a roller coaster and the Max Height.
roller_data |>
  drop_na(TopSpeed, MaxHeight)|>
            summarize(corr = cor(TopSpeed, MaxHeight))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
   corr
  <dbl>
1 0.904

#correlation for all numeric variables.
#Top results are fairly high positive relationships between Drop and Top Speed (0.91), Drop and Max height (0.95), and Top speed and Max height (0.90). No real relationships among other variables. Was surprised with the Year opened not having a relationship with most variables as I hypothesized newer roller coasters might be faster/higher.
roller_data |>
  select(`Year Opened`, TopSpeed, MaxHeight, Drop, Length, Duration, `# of Inversions`) |>
  drop_na() |>
  cor()

#numeric histogram with Top speed after creating object g3
g3 <-ggplot(roller_data, aes(x = TopSpeed)) 
g3 + geom_histogram() +
  labs(title = "Top Speed of Roller Coasters", x = "Top Speed (mph)")

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.


Warning: Removed 74 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_bin()`).








#numeric kernel density graph with Top speed and using Age as a fill
g3 + geom_density(alpha = 0.5, aes(fill = AgeF))+
  labs(title = "Top Speed of Roller Coasters", x = "Top Speed (mph)", y = "Count")

Warning: Removed 74 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_density()`).








#numeric boxplot graph with Top speed and using Age as a fill
g3 + geom_boxplot(aes(x = AgeF, y = TopSpeed, fill = AgeF)) +
  labs(title = "Top Speed of Roller Coasters", x = "Age", y = "Top Speed (mph) of Roller Coasters")

Warning: Removed 74 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).

g4 + geom_density(alpha = 0.5, aes(fill = AgeF))+

#numeric boxplot graph with Max height and using Age as a fill

#Creating object g5 then creating scatter plot between Top speed and Max height. Plot shows strong positive association that matches correlation above. 
g5 <-ggplot(roller_data |> drop_na(TopSpeed, MaxHeight), aes(x = TopSpeed, y = MaxHeight, color = MaxHeight)) 

#Higher roller coasters go faster scatter plot.
g5 + geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Higher roller coasters reach faster speeds", x = "Max Height (ft)", y = "Top Speed (mph)")







#Scatterplot between Top speed and Max height with faceting with 1 categorical variable (age of roller coasters). Newer roller coasters do tend to go faster based on the 3rd panel. 
g5 + geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Higher roller coasters reach faster speeds", x = "Max Height (ft)", y = "Top Speed (mph)") +
  facet_wrap(~AgeF)







#Scatterplot between Top speed and Max height with faceting with 2 categorical variables (age of roller coasters and scale of rating). 
#The plot is pretty busy with so many panels but you can see a little bit of a relationship between scale of rating and recent/newest roller coasters reaching taller heights.
g5 + geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Higher roller coasters reach faster speeds", x = "Max Height (ft)", y = "Top Speed (mph)") +
  facet_wrap(~AgeF + ~ScaleF)
